title: House of Many Ways - Chapter Two (Fragment 4)
text:
But that doesn’t help me wash dishes, she thought. Or about a bath. Am I supposed to boil myself in that tank? And where shall I sleep, for goodness’ sake?

Leaving the door open for Waif, she went back indoors, where she marched past the sink, the bags of laundry, the crowded table, and the heap of her own things on the floor, and dragged open the door in the far wall. Beyond it was the musty living room again.

“This is hopeless!” she said. “Where are bedrooms? Where is a bathroom?”

Great-Uncle William’s tired voice spoke out of the air. “For bedrooms and bathroom, turn left as soon as you open the kitchen door, my dear. Please forgive any disorder you find.”

Charmain looked back through the open kitchen door to the kitchen beyond it. “Oh, yes?” she said. “Well, let’s see.” She walked carefully backward into the kitchen and shut the door in front of her. Then she hauled it open again, with what she was beginning to think of as the usual struggle, and turned briskly left into the door frame before she had time to think of it as impossible.

She found herself in a passageway with an open window at the far end. The breeze coming in through the window was strongly full of the mountain smell of snow and flowers. Charmain had a startled glimpse of a sloping green meadow and faraway blue distances, while she was busy turning the handle and shoving her knee against the nearest door.

This door came open quite easily, as if it were used rather a lot. Charmain stumbled forward into a smell that caused her instantly to forget the scents from the window. She stood with her nose up, sniffing delightedly. It was the delicious mildewy fragrance of old books. Hundreds of them, she saw, looking round the room. Books were lined up on shelves on all four walls, stacked on the floor, and piled on the desk, old books in leather covers mostly, although some of the ones on the floor had newer looking colored jackets. This was obviously Great-Uncle William’s study.

“Oooh!” Charmain said.

Ignoring the way the view from the window was of the hydrangeas in the front garden, she dived to look at the books on the desk. Big, fat, redolent books, they were, and some of them had metal clasps to keep them shut as if they were dangerous open. Charmain had the nearest one already in her hands when she noticed the stiff piece of paper spread out on the desk, covered with shaky handwriting.

“My dear Charmain,” she read, and sat herself down in the padded chair in front of the desk to read the rest.

My dear Charmain,

Thank you for so kindly agreeing to look after this house in my absence. The elves tell me I should be gone for about two weeks. (Thank goodness for that!, Charmain thought .) Or possibly a month if there are complications. (Oh.) You really must forgive any disorder you find here. I have been afflicted for quite some time now. But I am sure you are a resourceful young lady and will find your feet here quite readily. In case of any difficulty, I have left spoken directions for you wherever these seemed necessary. All you need do is speak your question aloud and it should be answered. More complex matters you will find explained in the suitcase. Please be kind to Waif, who has not been with me for long enough to feel secure, and please feel free to help yourself to any books in this study, apart from those actually on this desk, which are for the most part too powerful and advanced for you. (Pooh. As if I cared for that!, Charmain thought .) Meanwhile I wish you a happy sojourn here and hope to be able to thank you in person before very long.

vocabulary.definition: Caused to suffer or be affected by something unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: I have been afflicted for quite some time now.

vocabulary.definition: In an active and energetic way.
vocabulary.example: Then she hauled it open again, with what she was beginning to think of as the usual struggle, and turned briskly left into the door frame before she had time to think of it as impossible.

vocabulary.definition: A device with a hinged metal fastener used to hold objects or parts together.
vocabulary.example: Big, fat, redolent books, they were, and some of them had metal clasps to keep them shut as if they were dangerous open.

vocabulary.definition: Difficulties or problems that make a situation more complex or challenging.
vocabulary.example: ) Or possibly a month if there are complications.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows great pleasure or joy.
vocabulary.example: She stood with her nose up, sniffing delightedly.

vocabulary.definition: A shallow, flat container for cooking or serving food.
vocabulary.example: But that doesn’t help me wash dishes, she thought.

vocabulary.definition: A state of confusion or lack of organization.
vocabulary.example: Please forgive any disorder you find.

vocabulary.definition: Something discovered as a result of investigation or search.
vocabulary.example: Please forgive any disorder you find.

vocabulary.definition: To pull or drag with effort.
vocabulary.example: Then she hauled it open again, with what she was beginning to think of as the usual struggle, and turned briskly left into the door frame before she had time to think of it as impossible.

vocabulary.definition: A pile or mound of things laid on top of each other.
vocabulary.example: Leaving the door open for Waif, she went back indoors, where she marched past the sink, the bags of laundry, the crowded table, and the heap of her own things on the floor, and dragged open the door in the far wall.

vocabulary.definition: Not able to occur or be done.
vocabulary.example: Then she hauled it open again, with what she was beginning to think of as the usual struggle, and turned briskly left into the door frame before she had time to think of it as impossible.

vocabulary.definition: At once; immediately.

vocabulary.definition: Clothes and linens to be washed or that have been washed.
vocabulary.example: Leaving the door open for Waif, she went back indoors, where she marched past the sink, the bags of laundry, the crowded table, and the heap of her own things on the floor, and dragged open the door in the far wall.

vocabulary.example: Charmain had a startled glimpse of a sloping green meadow and faraway blue distances, while she was busy turning the handle and shoving her knee against the nearest door.

vocabulary.definition: Having the smell or presence of mildew (a fungus).
vocabulary.example: It was the delicious mildewy fragrance of old books.

vocabulary.definition: A narrow path or corridor connecting different areas.
vocabulary.example: She found herself in a passageway with an open window at the far end.

vocabulary.definition: To arrange objects in a pile.
vocabulary.example: Books were lined up on shelves on all four walls, stacked on the floor, and piled on the desk, old books in leather covers mostly, although some of the ones on the floor had newer looking colored jackets.

vocabulary.definition: Strongly reminiscent or suggestive of something; fragrant.
vocabulary.example: Big, fat, redolent books, they were, and some of them had metal clasps to keep them shut as if they were dangerous open.

vocabulary.definition: Having the ability to find quick and clever ways to overcome difficulties.
vocabulary.example: But I am sure you are a resourceful young lady and will find your feet here quite readily.

vocabulary.definition: Unsteady or trembling; not firm or confident.
vocabulary.example: Charmain had the nearest one already in her hands when she noticed the stiff piece of paper spread out on the desk, covered with shaky handwriting.

vocabulary.definition: A temporary stay in a place.
vocabulary.example: ) Meanwhile I wish you a happy sojourn here and hope to be able to thank you in person before very long.

vocabulary.definition: Not easily bent or moved.
vocabulary.example: Charmain had the nearest one already in her hands when she noticed the stiff piece of paper spread out on the desk, covered with shaky handwriting.

vocabulary.definition: To trip or momentarily lose balance while walking or running.

vocabulary.definition: In need of rest or sleep; weary.

Great-Uncle William’s tired voice spoke out of the air.
